transcript.transcript: My name is William M. Brown. The M for Moses. My aka is Lew Brown. And it all emanates from being born in Hartford. Native son at 83. And I give consent to the professor for this interview. All right. Thank you. Yes. And so we won't pay attention to the camera if you can just pay attention to me. Let's start with your name. What's the origin story of that? Well, I found out that, Moses, the middle name, as a youngster, I didn't wrap my arms around the biblical name of Moses. And living in Bellevue Square at that time, the public housing development, which you've done some marvelous research on why it was built. If we got into trouble, if a police officer came to Bellevue Square and said, Luigi. You're in a black neighborhood. What do you mean, Luigi? I went by that name. And over the years, it went from the street name being little Lewie, Luigi, to the different spelling, and from L-U-I-G-I, which I still use to, Lew, L-E-W. And we associate L-E-W with the Jewish migrants. And are you a namesake for your father? No. My father's name was Calvin Agent, A-G-E-N-T, Brown. My father was born and raised in Denmark, South Carolina. He saw his father killed by ignorant white supremacists there in Denmark, South Carolina. My grandmother had three kids by Mr. Brown, my grandfather. And she always had a fear that my father would be hurt down there. And she got married a second time, but they moved from Denmark, South Carolina to Trenton, New Jersey. Because of the fear? Yes. And then the actual. Oh, my grandfather had like ten siblings or nine siblings. And they were told by the ignorant folks down there after my grandfather was killed that "y'all got to take the fastest thing moving out of here." So they all left Denmark, South Carolina and headed north. What sort of trumped up charge did they kill your grandfather for? My grandfather's nickname was Watermelon Brown. He had his own watermelon patch that he owned. The land, he owned the land. A road worker stole one of my grandfather's watermelons. His patch. My grandfather went to court. My grandfather was a no nonsense man. He saw the way it was going. And he'd came out. He came out what? He came out shooting. Ah. He saw the way the court was going. Well, the man who stole the watermelon, who worked for the road crew down there in Denmark, was a black man. My grandfather was a no nonsense man, God rest his soul. My father, a no nonsense man, hard working, God rest his soul. And I guess that's in my DNA. But, they left. He met my mom there in Trenton, New Jersey. She came from Georgia. There were ten siblings. I had nine siblings, I should say. I was the 10th. My oldest sister died in infancy there in Trenton, New Jersey. My oldest brother, who has just passed, he was born at Mercer County Hospital in Trenton, New Jersey. My father couldn't find work there in Trenton, and he came to Hartford to stay with some of my mother's relatives. That's how we ended up here in Hartford, Connecticut. Do you know what the original attraction to Trenton was? What was the connection to Trenton? It was part of the Great Migration. It was better than being in the south. Right. But Trenton is a very specific locale, right? Because folks are going to Chicago and they're going to New York. And why Trenton? I wish I could answer that with some with some validity, but in hindsight, I can't except to be servants. Well, to be servants. I moved to Trenton in my wandrous years, and I worked at Educational Testing Service, and the job I got was as a janitor. It was, as I remember Trenton, I said I couldn't stay here. I had to get out of here. It was like compared to Hartford. Hartford was no, but Trenton. So to answer your question directly, I can't tell you. They got jobs, finally and it was in the the state government. Even with the Archdiocese of Trenton, they were part of the politics. Does that sound familiar of Trenton, New Jersey? But at the same time, when it came to Catholicism, that's where I saw my first black nun of an order there in Trenton. But what do Black Americans or African Americans of Trenton, New Jersey, do at the time? I don't know. South Trenton, I can remember as a kid going to the bathroom at an out house in Trenton, New Jersey. The resiliency of Black/African Americans is something to behold. But, yeah, just one thing about Trenton, just lastly, I worked at the Educational Testing Service. I was going to live in Trenton, but I felt something. Like in your spirit or in your gut? In my spirit and my gut, my gut, my spirit and a combination of things. Then what really sealed it for me, like I said, I had a job at Educational Testing Service, and I was considering they, ETS, paid for night school at Princeton. I considered that. Then one day I went to a package store owned by a Jewish merchant, and I asked for a Heublein mixed drink. Now, Heublein is based in Farmington. And he said to me, "oh, you know about Heublein." I said, "yeah, my hometown." He said, "what?" He engaged me in conversation like the streets of Hartford were paved in gold. And I said, I'll never forget there was a clothing store, and that I was a clothing fanatic. And the clothing store was Roger Bannister. And you said you were clothing fanatic. Yeah. Okay. Clothing fanatic. And the style of the... Well, despite that, there were some draws there. And then coming from Hartford, just lastly, being a capital city. I was amazed. This is the capital city for Trention, for New Jersey, excuse me. Trenton is a capital city for New Jersey. For us? Wooh, I said, I gotta get out of here before I hurt somebody. So let's back up a little bit and do the paternal side of your family. How how far back can you go with this paternal side of your family? I can go back to my beautiful grandma. So we're talking about two generations. Okay. But because of the desire to find out more I went to a family reunion. That's what really excited me. About ten years ago, maybe. And I found out that it was for a weekend down in the greater Trenton area. And I found out that they had had skits on Friday night. We didn't get there till Saturday. Coming home from Hartford. We got there, and it was like we walked in and, they marveled at us. They were what? They marveled at us. Oh, the rest of the family? From Delaware other areas of Pennsylvania. I found out why: they did not know that the Browns had moved further north than New York and Philadelphia, that area, Delaware. And they were in tears like we were part of the lost tribe. So there wasn't a tradition of family reunions? [Shakes head] Well, we didn't know very many. I did not know about them. My older brother, four years older than me, he knew about them because he was born there in Trenton at Mercer County Hospital. So he knew the family that had traveled from Denmark, South Carolina to Trenton. Our grandmother, our paternal grandmother, kept us in touch. So, part of your family, Denmark, South Carolina, and then your father meets your mother, who's from Georgia. She's from Dawson. Georgia. Dawson, Georgia. And what do you know about the Dawson side of the lineage? Unfortunately, unfortunately, very little. I say that it bothers me to have to say that because when we would go to Trenton, my mother and father got married in Trenton, like I said. We had a cousin Nanny. Her name we called Cousin nanny or Aunt Nanny. She's my mother's aunt. My mother's family were more mixed than my father's family. Light skinned and the whole nine yards. But the connections were not like what the Brown side. And so we know very little. So, my wife has helped me because she's doing with her polish and learning more about my mother's side. I do remember as a child my mother taking me to Georgia. That when we had moved to Hartford for her father's going home service. I found his name, Pete Hill. I found out that he was mixed with my mother's aunt. We always thought that she was Native American because her whole look was Native American, light skin, the whole look. And in terms of, when we moved here, Hartford, we got to know people who weren't how should I say blood? But you know how it is when people any nationality, they move different strange places, cousins and so on and so forth. And we had that situation here in Hartford. But as far as blood is concerned, I couldn't honestly tell you based on research. Who did you think was Native American? So you said Pete Hill was was the father so his sister, the Aunt? No, Pete Hill was my father's, I mean, my mother's father. Right. But apparently he had several-- Sisters? He had several brothers. And it's one of those aunts. Your mother's aunt they thought was Native American? Yeah. In other words, we've never really established hard nosed based on research, factual, how far the lineage went. In fact, for instance, in my family, on my mother's side, her brothers and sisters, they were all either had the same father, different mothers, so on and so forth. And so we never established. We remember Uncle Bud, who was in the Navy, who was my mother's brother. We remember Uncle Willie B, who was in the army. Remember our Aunt Annie Gay. And we had another aunt. You said, Annie Gay? Yeah. And Uncle Bud? Uncle Bud, his name was John Perry. John Perry? John Perry. Willie B. Grimes, another uncle. I think what fires me up is taking your lead to research. That must be done. Once we know who we are-- Yeah. You stand tall. Yes. Did everybody move to Jersey? Uncle Bud, Willie Grimes, Annie Gay? Like I said, my mother moved here to Hartford on old Donald Street in North End to stay with her aunt. To do what? To to live with her aunt. Stay with her aunt. Okay. My father would come here, well, first, my father came here after a while. Oh, your mom came first? My mom came first. My father came after a while to work in the foundries. After being unable to find a job in-- Trenton. In Trenton? Let me back up. And I'm thinking about my mother and father lived there in Trenton. Yes. What decades? But what I'm saying is that, and now that I think about it, she stayed there in Trenton. I think he might have stayed with her aunt. Some relatives of hers who lived in the North End. Oh, he came first? He came first. He came first. To find work. Yeah, that's how I remember it. I stand to be corrected. But the whole point is that he couldn't find work there in Trenton. And he's a hard working man. And my grandmother was glad to get him out of Trenton because of the racial situation there in Trenton, New Jersey. And she knew that he had a temper like his father, and he wouldn't take any nonsense. And on my father's side, my grandmother, a strong Black woman. That meant what she her VU Meter never went above. She smoked a pipe every day. She read the Bible. I'm imagining her now. Was it one of those long pipes? Well, in fact, I think I got it. I got a pipe. I think a Cornhusker pipe. A real Cornhusker pipe. My grandmother was a strong woman. My paternal grandmother was a strong woman. So she smoked the pipe. And did what? Read her Bible every day and every night. Now, what's a VU Meter? When in the media, you know, how when you talk loud the meter [motions with finger going from side to side]. Ah, okay. She was very quiet, but effective. I never heard her raise her voice, but you listened to her. I called it quiet thunder. Where did she stay? She stayed in Jersey. So the Browns family was in Trenton, New Jersey. Until that family reunion. And your paternal grandmother, the one you said smoked the pipe. What's her name? Arrie, A-R-R-I-E-, Hayes. In fact, I have her wedding, the marriage certificate for my mother and father with her name on it. What's his name? Your grandfather? My grandmother. Arrie, A-R-R-I-E. And her husband? Calvin. Oh, that's Calvin's wife. Okay. Her husband's name was Calvin. My father was named after his father. My mother's father's name was Pete Hill, and my mother's mother's name was Lucinda Harrell, H-A-R-R-E-L-L. Harrell or Harrell? That's her maiden name. So she becomes Lucinda Harrell Hill. She becomes Lucinda Hill. My grandmother. Yeah. I don't know. We'll have to find out. I don't know. Hopefully we can look in Ancestry and try to put the pieces together. You've done a little bit of that work, right? I've done a little bit of that. I've done a little bit of it. And with each turn, I stand tall. But again I say to you, I believe in divine happenings. The fact that you came from Jamaica and your family moved to-- Trenton. To Trenton. Think about that. Where I'm coming from. Yeah. How does that happen? And then me meeting you later professionally because your research on the public housing development in Bellevue Square. You being a native son, I never knew that it was. I had no idea. But the hard core research, it was built for Black folks at a time, you know. So I give credit to you, that's why I give credit to you. I have a question for you about that. So, in my other life or my earlier life, um, I work on South African history. You didn't tell me that. That's my first book. Why don't I have your book? Your research on. I will bring you a copy. Why don't I have a copy of your research on Bloomfield. I will bring you a copy. Being the highest number of African-American or Black Americans property owners. Because it's all about the money. I will bring you the book, but it occurs to me, when I first started doing the research on Hartford, I thought I was going to have a mountain of books to go through to unpack the research, and there was almost nothing. I mean, I thought that my dining room table was going to be full with this publication and this publication, like ten of these. Because I was uncovering so much that I thought, certainly-- Does it have to do with the fact that Hartford, at the turn of the century, was one of the richest cities in the nation? Does it have anything to do with the fact that when in the 40s and 50s, when when Blacks came here from the South, like seeking the warmth of other suns. Even uneducated, not knowing. One of the things that used to stick in my claw was a theBlack presidents thing. There's something every year in the last 20 years. Is it like the black governors? The Black governors. Yeah. So I had a discussion again, me with a young lady. She said, "that's bullshit." I said, "what do you mean?" "They didn't govern anything. They were Uncle Toms." "What do you mean?" The Black governors wore the clothing of their white overseers here in Hartford. Hartford? She was right. She says, "who were they in charge here. Even in Hartford, certain areas." Does that sound like politics? Ward politics. That's what they had in Trenton: Ward Politics. Yeah, that's what they got in Bridgeport: Ward politics. Now what that meant to a native son, which I am. I'm a native son. Let me do my homework. At every turn I was learning more about me. At every turn. And when I learned because of your research. So to answer your question, was it part of the bullshit? Was it done intentionally? And then your research. I figure you're going down to the Old State House and finding out about the migrants who came here from Europe. But I also did my research into Thomas Hooker. And then constantly reading and learning about the Pilgrim factor and the Mayflower factor, and then learning about the White Lion that brought a cargo 20-30 Africans and landed in 1619, in Virginia, Colony of Virginia. And I got a good education here in Hartford. Never heard about that. A year before Nicole-Hannah Jones's book 1619, 1620 is when the pilgrims here but here in Hartford we never learned about why. And then thinking about was that intentionally done? How come we were never mentioned in the books, the school books? In fact, I ended up going to one of my teachers at Hartford High, which where I got my education. I also liked the swashbuckle. And I learned like Three Musketeers, Dumas. I went back after I graduated, when I learned about Dumas, I went to him and said his name was Alfred Scott Smith, acronym A.S.S. And I said to him, you know, whether you intend or not, you really were an ass. He said to me, Brown. You mean for leaving out? Dumas. The fact that his mother, and he always identified, and then going to, slow down now, going to Paris and going to the island where the Count of Monte Cristo and all that. They find out that he never denied his mother's heritage. He was he was no nonsense. But to hear that somebody who is Dumas-- You read the work and don't know you're reading the work of a Black person. A Black person, you could see pictures, but never read based on facts that his mother was African. But you'll never read Russian literature and not find out that this person is Russian, right? That's a part of the story. The heritage, the origins, the part of the story. So getting back to, Hartford, I'm wondering now, was it accidentalwhy you couldn't find material here in Hartford? And we had bright learned people. The thing is the material, the raw materials are there. And so one of the things that happens is that when I meet other folks, including folks in your generation, who've told many other researchers and many other Trinity students or U-Hart students, so that by the time I show up, there's this fatigue about saying the same things over and over again. We keep meeting with researchers and talking to researchers and then-- Nothing. Six years, ten years later, some new student discovers the same thing. Yeah. I've had students from Trinity and UConn for instance. And I've told them certain things and I respect them. But like you said in academia. Trinity College, gee, in my generation, I can remember when Trinity College might as well have been out in Farmington, somewhere out in Avon. Then you do your research and you find out who established, who founded Trinity College. Well, then what happens is that Hartford would attract the best. And the Southerners would send their kids up here to be educated. And then what happens is, in the 50s with the Great Migration of Jamaicans, I say a great deal of pride, love and understanding. We're able to avoid the BS because of Black Americans, in my opinion. What do you mean? The brainwashing. They were able to hold on more. My response to it is that they were able to come here as immigrants on their own free will. Whereas my ancestors were brought here as slaves. Now, they might have some West Indian background, but they were brought here slaves. And that's all we knew. So today fast forward I say even in my age, I'm still learning, thank God. In my church at Saint Justin-Saint Michael, we were brought here on different ships from Alabama, Georgia, Mississippi, the Cape Verde Islands, from Jamaica, Puerto Rico. We were brought to Hartford on different ships, but politically we're in the same boat now. Try to create or compete with generational wealth. They wrote the book. I'm always learning. My visit to the Old State House, I learned about the migrants from you. I learned about the migrants. And so here's my thinking, Thomas Hooker came here and settled. He discovered, he ain't discovered shit, and settled in Hartford. After their bloodline ran out, they turned to politics, elected politics through the Jewish immigrants. Who stepped aside in the sense of going below ground. They gave the politics over to, and this is me, to the Italians, because they came here in large numbers. And then, of course, they mingled with the Irish. Now what happens? What I'm saying is that today the family heritage, no anger, but that's the way it went. And in my opinion, I wish I could do the research to find out behind the scenes. For instance, Governor Ned Lamont, his ancestors, about 17 generations away or whatever, came over on the Mayflower. Or came to Connecticut, to Hartford, with Thomas Hooker. His ancestors did. That kind of research would explain, based on, in my opinion, based on the politics of Hartford today. And you say, look, what good would that do? Factual research. You know knowledge is power. Knowledge is power. And what is the expression today? We today are living on an oasis of knowledge, drowning in a pool of ignorance. Who said that? There's been many, that quote has been attributed to many people, including Marcus Garvey. So, if you do a deep dive, there are many quotes like that quote attributed to certain people. And then doing my research about Marcus Garvey: he was damned. In Jamaica, Marcus Garvey was damned in Jamaica. He was damned in this country, in many cases in this country. Like Jamaica, he was damned by his own people. That's not just hearsay, that's fact. But that's not fact. I should not say it, because that's what I read. And I stand to be corrected. That quote I got from my son, who's African American, Black American, Polish American, French American. He's a lieutenant in the state judicial system. And I did my research. He gave he gave credit to a young rock star or young rapper. Who was Dominican. He gave credit to a young Dominican who was the founder of the, I'm losing my thoughts, the gang, not the Savage Nomads, but the other well-known Latino gang. The Lords, the young Lords? The Kings. The Lation King. Oh, the Latin kings. The Young Lords, the brother who, I can't remember his name, but I was in touch with him, with the Young Lords. Whenever there is some structure that simulated with a black or brown perspective, of, a lack of better term, of white folks, I don't like to use that term because of the fact that. Which term? Assimilate? White folks because it lets people off the hook. It it allows people who are to say, "oh, you're a racist" or whatever, but that's just me. But I like to be specific here in Hartford. The immigrants, their attitude, not just the Irish and Italians. But our West Indian brothers and sisters were immigrants who came here through on their own will through Canada or whatever. They can't figure out Black Americans. But I put those in capital letters: Black Americans. When I came here and everything is historical, there's a historical perspective. Like as I find out from you, my sister, that Bellevue Square was built for Black folks. We thought it was-- It was all done out of ignorance. For me to find out about it, and you don't charge for psychological. How I found out about it was as a reporter, going to the disturbances of the 60s, being born and being a native son, working at WGAA radio. That night, going out after the murder of Doctor King that I had credibility. How long had you been working at the radio? In the midst of-- '68. So just a few years before that? Yeah. I went out that night, professor. And I had credibility as a reporter. And I was working only part time there because full time in the post office, my bread and butter. So what happened was I went out that night. It was my first marriage, and I lived in Westbrook Village. Hit the streets, things were burning and the whole nine yards. Go to the north to the Hartford-Windsor line. A barricade of cops. Protecting Windsor? Protecting? Yes. I could assume that. But what happened, I am barricaded by cops saying to me, "Lew, you can't go no farther than here." Which I didn't want to go any further than there, but its a barricade of cops. Go to the south of Hartford, Trumbull and Main Street: a barricade of cops. "Lew you ain't going down the south end." On the west side was Albany Avenue and Homestead. On the east side-- That's Albany and Homestead is where the barricade was? Oh, yeah. On the west, on that side? On the west side. On the east side. What do we have. The north end with the railroad tracks ain't nobody living over there. The Connecticut River. No cops there. So then, I'm getting emotional about it. I got home that night, I woke up in the middle of the night: "Shit. We are living on a plantation." This is me in the middle of the night, waking up it dawned on me, what they were saying to us without verbalizing it, you can burn anything you want in that area, you ain't coming any further than this. But we didn't realize and I didn't realize it. And I was street wise. It occurred to me that. So that's my reality. It was a wake up call. That's why I know I'm blessed, though, because innate intelligence. But we were living on a plantation and did not realize. Can you imagine? Then consider 60 years later, federal money came in for the, what was it the promise zone? Oh, recently? Yeah, the promise zone. Right. I took Andrew Woods to the window at 2550 Main Street when that money came in and said, "man, look out this window here." He left his place at 2550 Main Street. What's at 2550 Main Street? The building there. There's the church. Phillips? Metropolitan? Yeah. But the church, that building right next to it was once a state owned building. So state money built that building. But what my point is, though, professor, is that 60 years later, money came in from the federal government for that particular area, the promise zone. Check the map out. To the point where I went to a community meeting and different nonprofits were trying to decide who can get the money, right. Open. I mean, they were not being nefarious. I guess that's the word. Finally, this money is coming into this area. Finally, 60 years. And some who came from the Blue Hills area were told this money is designated for Black and brown folks in the North End. But my point being is that it was specific where that money could be spent at and how it could be spent. Nonprofits were told. In fact, there was one member of a nonprofit, at the meeting, made some statements that were not true. Apparently, because the young lady who was working for Mayor Bronin at the time told her at that meeting, a crowd of people from Blue Hills, all over, told her, you're not being honest. You came into my office, and I told you that that money could not be utilized by the Blue Hills Civic Association because of the Promise Zone. If you look at the map, the Promise Zone didn't include-- Upwards of Albany. And the young lady who was there, had her baby, bright young sister. And she had to sit down. I told you when you came to talk to me that this is so. The young lady in the mayor's office knew the law, and she was not getting involved in the gray area. Emotionally, perhaps I should include. But for me, things have changed over the years and we ended up with gerrymandering that has been done by the Democrats, by the way. You end up with pockets of Black and brown people who came here from different cultures. And some understood that the melanin in our skin is what binds us. When you say so, what are the areas? So what's the relationship between Main Street, Upper Albany, Barber Street, Blue Hills? Well, generationally for me being 83 years of age when I lived in Bellevue Square, you've been in Bellevue Square, when I lived in Bellevue Square if you go up Mahl Avenue, right across Main Street there, that was like moving on up. Going west, that was like moving on up. The predominantly Black area in the east side was known as the bottom. Ironically enough, I learned that a section of Detroit, Michigan is called that. Now, what is it the bottom of in Hartford? Why is it called the bottom? At that time, from what it was for me, was that there were migrants from Europe, from all over, there were Black Americans, mainly from the south in large numbers, or Chicago or wherever, and there was a few numbers of Caribbean. But, when they landed in Hartford, they couldn't find rooms or whatever further west. I was always a curious kid. Like. Like where the 29th, where they're buried up there at that cemetery. That was like coming from the side, you never mess with dead people. But that area there, if you go back far enough it starts making sense that the S.A.N.D. School that was known as the Arsenal School. The Arsenal, yeah. Well, there was an Arsenal from the Civil War. That's why it was named Arsenal School. The building, the brownstone buildings next to the S.A.N.D. School, going north, right adjacent, were known as the Widows Home. It was a home for civil rights widows. So I'll just say I learned more with the help of research. And you like to put the icing on the cake because I'm too emotionally attached. I'm just too remote. I don't like to play intellectual scrabble. Like I said, with the ignorance of white supremacy, nor do I suffer fools lightly. But you've got that ease about you. That calmness about you. And one of your colleagues who I didn't care for has now since passed. The white colleague that has who wants to take credit for you being down at the Old State House. I don't suspect. What was that boy's name? Owsley. I don't suspect he thought you were that deep. I don't suspect he thought that you would go that deep. Now, God bless you. But I'm saying to you, that's another reason why now I understand it. I accomplished more than I ever thought I could accomplish coming from Bellevue Square at that particular time. Being in a public housing development, over 500 apartments. I fooled the devil. I fooled the devil. Yeah. Let's talk about that a little bit. So what year did you move to Hartford? I was born here in Hartford. Right. In 1942, I was born in Hartford Hospital in 1942. At that time, we lived, my parents apparently lived on the old Donald Street. And they moved. They got apartments there at Bellevue Square. Okay. We lived in 49 Bellevue Square. In a garden apartment. A garden apartment. What's a garden apartment? Well, the configuration was, your front door was outside in a play area where there was grass. And so, in fact, I thought about one of the things that made me strong, I think about the way the environment was there. There was, like I said, the previous generation of Black folks, mainly from the South, they looked out, we looked out for each other. There was a Black woman by the name of Mama Lou. Mama Lou. I was a scrawny little kid. She would order milk for me when the milkman came in Bellevue Square. She made sure a quart of milk was left for me. She paid for it. Her name was Mama Lou. Mama Lou and Papa Lou lived in building 50, five-zero. That was the environment I was brought up in. Do you know what folks have told me about that is they were cold water flats. Tenement buildings. Old tenement buildings. That whatever Bellevue Square became, whatever people thought Bellevue Square was, or whatever the actual intentions were to congregate and hurd people, that that doesn't tell the whole story, because you can do that. You can hurd and congregate and segregate people, but that doesn't become their entire experience. Because for them, from cold water flats and tenements to Bellevue Square still represented an upgrade at the time. An opportunity-- Listen. Let me testify. Well, listen, we had the other alternative was when my mom and dad came and they lived on old Donald Street. Cold water flats that were formerly lived in full numbers by the immigrants. Let me tell you a very interesting story. There was a late judge by the name of Arthur Spada, S-P-A-D-A, who came here from Italy, and they lived down on the east side, they lived in cold water flats. So one day I'm at one of his speeches. He became a very successful judge, but he was a down to earth guy. This gathering was, because I used to shine shoes, a shoe shine boy, so the gathering was for former shoeshine boys who got the license to be shoeshine boys. And that's where I learned a lot of Italian. [Speaks in Italian]. So, what happens is he spoke because he's a former shoeshine boy. He said: "when I bought my house up on Prospect Avenue," which he and his wife did, he said I had ten, or how did he say it, he had five full baths and ten half baths. The way he explained it was, he said, when we lived on Oakland Street. We were an outie, o-u-t-i-e. I said to him later, "what do you mean you were an outie?" He said, "our bathroom it's outside of our apartment down the hall, and we shared it with other people we didn't know." So it humanized for me others have gone through the same thing. Other poor folks. But, we depend on their empathy. And then what happens to it? Well, it comes to human nature in my opinion. And that's why I respect Bill Carson so much. Because he's prioritized our humanity with that historical research that he's done.. Lets that other generation to know, like my grandkids, my great-grandkids, they'll never know, nor do I want them to have that. That's at a human level. And, so. it's natural you don't want your kids to go through the same, whether you be Irish, no matter your race, color. How come it hasn't happened? That's the question you ask me. That's the question that I have in my own mind. How come? How come we have a situation today? When history tells us they came for. the Jews, or they came for the whatever that Mueller, Martin Niemöller or something says. They came for the gypsy, I wasn't a gypsy. I said nothing. They came for on and on down the line, his last thing is that they came for me. There was nobody there to defend me. So today, they're rehashing the Civil War. And despite the laws, despite the 13th, 14th and 15th amendments, voting rights. But I got faith. The majority of people, especially like when people tell me "what about Jasmine Crockett?" She's set a new mold, intellectually and otherwise. It's a fact. Jasmine Crockett, Congresswomanman Crockett, got into a debate, not a screaming, pissing match, calm and collected like Professor Vernal, having done her research intellectually, said as I got it, not disrespectful, said to Sotomayor said to her, "laws were made about people. Those laws that you're talking about. You're dealing with now as a Supreme Court justice. They are there because of people. Have you forgotten the people? Oh, we must not forget the people." She was not combative. Oh, she was so smooth. Jasmine Crockett. Guess what happened to Sotomayor? Intellectually, she backed off. She backed off. But she was allowed to back off by Jasmine Crockett, telling her based on the historical perspectives. Alright, so when did you go back to Jersey? So born in 42, in Hartford. When what time period were you in New Jersey? When I was in my wanderlust phase when I was in my 20s. And I worked at Educational Testing Service. So what made you go to Jersey? Because something was not, I was just moved by a spirit. That's the only way I can explain it. I was moved by a spirit that higher power had a designation for me because I had been a wannabe gang member. The whole nine yards. And when I came back to Hartford, I took the test to be a letter carrier and I passed it. But so what made me come back to Hartford was: one, I'd been to Boston, try to get a job in Boston, couldn't get a job in Boston at the time because the I lived in the Back Bay area with my mother's sister and I couldn't get a job. I went to get a job to wash dishes. Nothing was below me. And there was the same time the the Boston Strangler was active there. I'll never forget I went down to the Porthouse, the Port Steakhouse to get a job washing dishes. The guy says, "you got the job, but you're gonna have to wait two more weeks before we could put you on the payroll." Hire you and put you on payroll because of extensive background checks. And later I thought to myself, if I had been Irish, I would have got that job in Boston to wash dishes. And then I wound up going to Trenton. And stay with my cousins on Bellevue Avenue. And, I would have stayed there except for the fact that-- I lived on Bellevue. That is the first place I lived. You're talking about five points down there in that area of, what is it? Pennington Avenue? Or further out Bellevue Avenue, way out in the more like Blue Hills Avenue areas. More like this area. Pennington? Pennington? Close to Junior Five. Okay. Close to Junior Five. At that time, what do you call it? Five points, Pennington? They all came together. Yeah. The different avenues there. That end of Bellevue Avenue is where we live at. That's my cousins. I can't believe we lived on the same street. But there's something. There's something happening. Isn't that something. You lived on Bellevue Avenue. Hold on one second. My baby sister, she stayed in Trent for a while as well. I'm only doing this here, as a newsman, you verify or you let people know you ain't just blowing smoke. My brain is starting to leave me. I'll put her on speaker. [Making phone call] It's about 2, 4:14, give me a call back. I want to run something by you. And I've got a young lady here who lived on Bellevue Avenue in Trenton, New Jersey. So, me, Donna and Bob are the last from that generation. Siblings. Donna is 73. They spent time in Trenton in their generation on Bellevue Avenue and had friends. Let me ask you one more question. The Carver Center. Does that sound familiar to you? The Carver Center? C-A-R-V-E-R. Named after George Washington Carver. That's where that area there was where the Blacks lived in that area. And the George Carver Center. Let me let me make another phone call. Just one more minute. My older brother, my brother is up in the Shriver compound playing golf. He was the first of my family to go to college. Went to Junior Five and then Trenton High. I gotta remember that, I got to start writing stuff down. Junior Five. So I'm not. I don't want you to be embarrassed. Are you? The reason I'm doing this here, that shows again, I'm too emotional. I thought it was bad enough. I got enough in terms of research because of you. [Making another phone call]. My brother, I hope you got to the golf course. Give me a call. I got some information for you regarding a young lady who, from Trenton, new Jersey. Man lived on not only lived in Trenton, she lived on Bellevue Avenue. Hello. God is trying to tell us something. Love you man. Yeah. Small worlds. Huh? We live next door to, I don't remember, the name of the family, but we live next door to some attorneys. You were up in Bellevue Avenue. And then. Across up way past Calhoun Street. Yeah. In the park area? Not so close to the park, but that was our park. Yeah. And then my sister and I used to walk to Junior Five. How did you, if you don't mind me asking, did your family move there? I was 12. Oh, why Trenton? My mother, so there's three of us on my mom's side. There's two older sisters. You don't mind me asking? I'm just curious. No, not at all. So I have two older sisters, like, just 18 months and then two years. And she had the pleasure and terror of having three girls. As a father of three girls. You have three girls and then they get their period and you're like, "oh, they're going to get pregnant." So the three girls and also the running saying at the time is, "well, you have three girls, maybe one of them will turn out to be something," The other two might get pregnant, one might go to university, and my mom bet on all three of us. She said no, because she's a very smart woman and she didn't get to go to school. So she bet to bet on all three of us, and she said, "no, all three of my girls are going to get an education." And so we all take those exams across the Caribbean to go to high school. And my mom was paying three tuitions, three bus fares. And it was hard. It was really hard. Let me say this to you. That same pattern existed within the Caribbean community amongst Jamaicans mainly. Education. But one day, it had to be in the 70s. In the 70s and it was a story about Weaver High School. Yeah. No, the new Weaver. I said because of the dates. So it's up this young West Indian brother who was acting out. And his sister said to him, "junior, I'm going to tell mama on you. Junior." Junior, the brother cussed out his sister. I walked up to him and said, "don't you ever speak to your sister that way in front of me." I'll never forget it "Junior, I'm gonna tell Mama." "Don't ever disrespect your mother." He looked at me like, who is this? I was very serious. Looked him it the eye. "Apologize to your sister, brother. Don't embarrass her." And he did. I've had situations like that as a newsman. When I met with some Catholic Keney Park gang members. Young gang members. Different generation. In fact, one of the guys impregnated my granddaughter or my daughter, excuse me, my daughter. Wolcott, last name, but grew up in Keney Park. Now, at that time, my thing was being macho. He asked me to come up here trying to settle something, right? I went up there and I was surrounded by the gang members with my camera from Channel 3 news. And there's one guy who was blowing smoke. Myself and Carl Hardrick, who's now at the Institute that the young Jamaican brothers had. And I had been invited up there. So he's smoking the ganja throwing in my face. And I said to him, I looked at him in his eyes. I said, "brother, today is a good day to die. I'm ready to die. If you blow one more bit of that smoke in my face." He was bigger than I. Is this with the camera rolling? I never would go with the camera rolling. See, to me it was like the camera was an intrusion. But I saw some reporters, mainly white and photographers use that camera to do things in our community that they wouldn't do in a suburban community. So anyhow, so I never go to camera. My granddaughter's father who just passed, Bruce Wolcott, about four months ago, maybe longer than that, said to me, "Pa." I said, "no, Bruce will you try to let me handle this?" Because Bruce was there will Carl Hardrick. And Bruce said, "the man is serious, he'll take somebody with him." And i meant it. What were you settling? They were fighting against each other. The gangs, street gangs. And I had knew the law enforcement angle of it, what they were doing. And they were fighting for street corners. To sell. When I went with my camera it was never to exploit anybody. Most of my people. But that's how the media worked. So were you settling the turf between the two of them? Two sides? Yeah. The ones we had. Yeah. Mhm. With Carl Hardrick there. Then later what happened was I don't know who called him, but a guy by the name of Jimmy Berry who was a sergeant or a captain then, Jimmy Berry. They came up and they were in a peripheral. I didn't know anything about that. They were in the what? In the area? Oh, okay. So, I'm laughing because Berry said "we ain't going to tell you to call you," but he said, "are you crazy, man?" I used to be one of them young brothers. Wannabe gang member, a gang member fighting for turf. Fighting for turf, you see? Oh, I think I'm just thinking about now it was back in the day with with guys like with less pain in them about the drugs and shit. I used to set my camera on the corner of Burton in Albany Avenue and see who was buying the drugs. Yeah. School bus drivers, folks leaving Hartford heading west on route 44, Albany Avenue, route 44. You could buy and them brothers were killing themselves. In fact, there's a young brother who we never solved his murder who was killed 16 or 17 years old. In fact, his sister, his sister is the elected board member. Shontay, in Hartford. She's elected board member in Hartford. I can't remember his name. But she lost her brother when he was sixteen. Well, he was selling drugs. And the rival drug members killed him. The rival drug members. The last I know, because it could have been some rogue cops. See back at that time, there was so much money flowing, Professor. But my point is that there was nothing for me to be up there, and because I was a wannabe, a different generation, but I understood the street and I never thought short because I can't explain that now. I think about it, "Negro are you crazy?" But when I said to that guy "it's a good day to die." And how did it end? He didn't do it again. I said, now, Bruce, you called me here, what do you want me to do? Who do you want me to talk to?" Bruce Wolcott, W-O-L-C-O-T-T. Like I said, he was my oldest grandchild's father or sperm donor. So '42 to '60, 1942 to 1960. Tell me what that those years were like for you? You're born, you're coming of age in Bellevue. Fond memories. You got Mama Lou? Yeah. The other families. Who else do you recall in Bellevue Square? Mrs. Ella Cromwell, Mr. Tillman, Ben Tillman family, the Gaddy family, the Martin family, the Hightowers, the Portillas, the Mendezes, the Mendezes were Cape Verdeans. They were Creole Catholics. Prior to them, the Black Catholics who attended the Saint Benedict, they came from Louisiana. [Speaking to April off camera] Okay. Yes, hun. April! Come, I want to introduce you to our visitor. The professor is taking notes. The Portilla's Uh. The Lees. Hold on one second. You said black Catholics. And what other kind of Catholics? No, they were Creole Catholics where they were born into Catholicism. Okay. Cape Verde islands or Puerto Rico? Right. The largest denomination were Catholics, Roman Catholics. The Portillas. And then? Well, more with the black Catholic angle, is that the McBrides they're in Bellevue Square. The Hightowers, The McBrides, they were the Black Catholics. But the other families that came from like the Portillas and so on and so forth. And the Arangos, A-R-A-N-G-O. So, where are they from? Cape Verde. It's just like up at our church now the largest number of family of 1 or 2 generations is from Saint Lucia. Understandably, with the government of Saint Lucia the Catholic diocese is strong. And how did you roam Hartford? Where did you go? Um. In age ten and twelve? All over. Are you a free range kid? Free range kid. Happy go lucky. Easy go free. Okay. Nothing in the world bothers me. Like your parents let you go? You're gone, but you have to come home at sundown? At the time, when the street lights came on, you were home. Okay, so street light curfew. Street light curfew. Gone all day. Okay. And I mean, if I wanted at that time lemon ice? Down the south end. That's a long way to go. Listen, walking was no problem. Can you imagine, I was a letter carrier. Yeah. And I would be remiss if I didn't say to you when I was into high school, sixth grade, actually, I was diagnosed with heart trouble, liver trouble, kidney trouble, and so on and so forth. I ended up in a bed at Hartford Hospital for a month. And how old were you then? 12 years old. 12. That I remember that fact. So then a month at Hartford Hospital in a bed in isolation. Then I went to a convalescent home down in Hamden Children's Center at 1400 Whitney Avenue in Hamden. I spent 11 months down there. Oh. I lived in a bed. Continual live in a bed. Talk about a bedpan and a urinal, the whole nine yards. That's was the year. That was the medical procedure at that time. That's where I had my first taste of a Italian pizza in Hamden. And then what was the recovery like? You recovered fully? I recovered fully. And plus I was always so exposed to the medical delivery service at that time, which was so few of us of color. Believe it or not. That received treatment at the level I received. That's why I know I'm blessed I received the best treatment in the world. There was no number game or a loss of ignorance or something. That's the problem today. But that's another story. What happened was I got exposure outside of Bellevue Square and saw people just it sounds corny judging people by the content of their character, not by the color of their skin. I mean you really judge people by the content of their character and their attitude towards you, the whole nine yards. I did that at Children's Center there at 1400 Whitney Avenue. And so then I came back here and I was in special ed class down at the school. I'm losing the name, the school down the south end. It'll come to me. The Naylor School. The James H. Naylor School. How do you spell the last? N-A-Y N-A-Y L-O-R Okay. The James H. Naylor School. Where did my wife go? I'm not sure, I don't see her outside. She's probably sitting outside. She's probably doing some studying. But I want her to meet before you leave. But anyhow. Is she leaving? No, I don't know. What time is it? It's 4:30. Wow. 4:35. Wow. But anyhow, no, we got nothing planned. So what happened was the Naylor School, then I was able to as an adult, the Naylor School is on the line between Hartford and Wethersfield. Wethersfield, yeah. I know for a fact that some kids from Wethersfield attended Hartford's Naylor School. Which meant it was top notch. Right. So, you were at the Naylor School because you hadn't been in school for more than a year. Yeah. And they wanted you to catch up? Well yeah. Plus, they had a class for handicapped kids at the Naylor School. Okay. And were you handicapped when you came back from Hamden? Oh, yeah. The cardiac, what I had was so serious some people died. What did you have? I had heart trouble, rheumatic fever, liver trouble and kidney trouble as a kid. That's a lot. One of those would be a lot. Yeah. So what happened was that they had this and I lived in the North End. Did you come back in a wheelchair? No. Crutches? No. Okay. I can remember the day I was allowed to get out of bed. It took me 24 hours to get my street legs. Okay. Well, my sea legs, I guess they call it. I mean, I went to school in a bed. That was the protocol at that time. Yeah. So come back here went to the Naylor School because they had a class there for handicapped kids. There were kids with polio, muscular dystrophy and wheelchairs, cerebral palsy and cardiac conditions. And in the north end there was myself, a Jewish boy by the name of Harold Bronstein, Bromberg. Harold Bromberg. Who lived in the north end at that time and he had muscular dystrophy. And there was a fellow by the name of Arthur Gaines, Junior Gaines, who lived there in Bellevue Square, who had muscular dystrophy. And there was a brother by the name of Walter McBride who had a cardiac condition, who's since, they were all passed on. And like a limousine will come in the north end and pick us up and take us to school. All of you in one limo? In one limo. Stretch limo to take us down to Naylor School. A Limousine, huh? A limousine. And then how long did you have to convalesce there? Oh, no. Well, I was home at that time. Okay. I was back here in Hartford. So just day school? Just day school Okay. And there was a woman I will never forget her name was Mrs. Margaret Johnson, who was in charge of it. And she was with the Board of Education, she's in charge of the kids down there at the school. But we also were allowed to see a different world. I was able to see a different world. And be exposed to it, right. And because of that that improved my perspective about people moving towards judging people by the content of their character, not by the culture. Then what happens is that when I returned to the public schools in Hartford, I went to Northeast Junior High School. Did you stay at Naylor for one year? Two years? Two years. Okay, I'm not sure, but I think it was two years. But even during that time, how things make an impression upon your youth, I went to Harkness Memorial State Park. Where's that? Down in Waterford. Okay. An old estate of the founders of the Standard Oil of New Jersey. Imagine that was still intact, but their bloodline had died out, so they gave it to the state. Back then, I can't remember the exact years, but what happened was that I saw a different world, I was exposed to a different world again. The one paper I did for my class half semester at the University of Hartford. I wrote about my experience of leaving the city where you could see the heat coming from the sidewalk. There was a summer camp, a summer camp for handicapped children with muscular dystrophy, handicapped with cerebral palsy. There were two camps. There was Harkness and then there was Camp Hemlock. Where's that one? I think it that one was up in eastern Connecticut, I think. Hemlock. But, of course, Harkness is down in Waterford, like I said. So exposed, my exposure. You said leaving Hartford and seeing the heat from the sidewalk. The radiation. You could see it. Yeah. And I wrote about it. I got an A on that paper. And then going to Harkness, what was Harkness like? Cool. Cool? Down by the shore. The mansion was still intact. What was intact? The mansion. It was a mansion. Oh, the okay. Standard Oil of New Jersey. It was on the, in fact, Harkness Memorial State Park if you go there sometime in the summer, they have programs or whatnot. But it was my exposure that's what it was I'm thinking of talking to you now. And what happened was that, I can never forget that in that mansion, there was a garage where they would park their limousine. They had a limousine in the garage. They would go in and then there was a pulley that you pull that limousine while heading that way, because there was no exit, it would turn and go out the same way it came in. There was another situation there where there were painting of the waterfowl on the road, I mean, on the walls. Ducks and the whole nine yards. And there were paintings that were done that were purchased by the people who lived there by Rex, R-E-X, Brasher, B-R-A-S-H-E-R. And in that mansion, now they had cabins for us, but we would go to that mansion and in that mansion there was a dumbwaiter. And that was still being used because it was so soon after the last of that family left it intact. And then they had a garden and the gardener was still there. He was Japanese. Had an oriental garden that was utterly unbelievable. That kind of exposure enlightened me. That's why I know I'm blessed. I had to get away. So before all of your conditions sent you off to the hospital, what was Hartford like? What were you thinking as a young kid? And you're roaming Hartford with your friends? I loved it. What did you love about it? I loved the environment and always being very close with friends. Loved it and still to this day. I love the city of my birth. And dislike the politics. Well, let's stay with the ten year old. Okay. Where are you buying candy? In the local stores. Mrs. Wilson, which was right next door to the Arsenal School. Mrs who? Wilson, W-I-L-S-O-N. And what was the name of the shop? Wilson's. Okay. Then when Mrs. Wilson, the Wilson family sold it, they sold it to the Pittmans, P-I-T-T-M-A-N. Did they change it to Pittman's? They kept it, as I can remember. This is a convenience store or candy shop? It was a candy store. Cuz you could gett any kind of candy you wanted. What did you buy? Squirrel nuts. And Good & Plenty and a bit of honey, yellow with a little red. A lot of those candies they sell them now on the top shelf and different places. But memories. So the memories. What else? The memories. Did you eat hot dogs? Hot dogs, it was tradition to have hot dogs on the weekends. In the north end, there were soul restaurants. That's why I use the term a hamlet and knowing what the old English, the hamlet of Bellevue Square located in the village of North East Hartford. ZIP code 06120 and 06107. In the fifth and seventh Assembly District. Things have changed with the districts, with the gerrymandering that went on amongst the Democrats. Let's stay at age ten. Where'd you play and what did you play? Played in Bellevue Square. It was self-contained. There was a playground there. There was a playground at the north side, a playground on the south side. There was a sandbox. There was a slide. In the middle between where the swings were, there were swings, in the middle was the whole conference was called the playground. There was a shower, in my generation a shower. At night we used to-- You said there was a pool? No, there was a shower. There was a shower. Today what would they you call it? A splash? Yeah, like a splash pad. It was a shower splash. And what we would do at night, they had a wall around it. Okay. What we would do at night was put linoleum over the drain in the summer and by the time the morning came we had a swimming pool. What else was in there? Now, Arsenal School at that time had a swimming pool. Arsenal school had a swimming pool? It had a swimming pool. The fellow who was in charge of it was a man by the name of Livingston. Livingston, who lived up on Greenfield Street. He's in charge. He was a gym teacher there for boys at the Arsenal School. You know where Mr. Livingston lived? We would walk from Bellevue Square or where it stands now, up to Keeney Park. Keeney Park was our playground. Now, to us it was not a long walk, but it was a way. The only problem we had, if we had a problem, was that cops would ask us, "what are you doing up here?" Because you understand, it was the Italians, the Irish, the Jewish. The North End even today, if you look at the churches, oh, no, I shouldn't say that because they've all been torn down and rebuilt. They were Star of Davids on the outside. The Jewish immigrants welcomed when I was a kid, welcomed us into the North End. As in, like, welcomed you into their stores? Their establishments? They were makeing money. But also on on Wooster Street, I remember that the Orthodox Jews on Friday night, I think they were Orthodox. Orthodox Jews don't turn on lights and all that jazz. I had a friend, I knew someone-- On Wooster Street? Well, I know someone who looked like me who got got a penny. Oh, to-- Turn the lights on or whatever. Because of their religion the Orthodox Jews can't do. Very interesting story, is that the Ebony Horsewomen. What's her sister's name? Anyhow, she lived on. Pat? Pat. Pat lived in the North End. And her next door neighbor was a Jewish merchant who went door to door who had a horse. Okay. That's why she became interested in horses. That's how she, yeah, I mean, in my generation. And, like, Pat was one of those who went to the Marines. Yeah. But her father was a deacon in a church. I think it was Hopewell Baptist Church. Pat Lawson, that was her maiden name, L-A-W-S-O-N. She lived in that area up on Martin, I don't know if you know Harvard well, but Capen and Martin Street up there, that was all Jewish. Martin Street, all Jewish and Italian. Mahl Avenue, mixed Italian and Jewish. So you're playing in Keeney Park wondering what? Bellevue Square. Bellevue Square. Initially. Well, we ventured out as we would to Keeney Park was like we loved it. What are you doing in the summer as a young kid in Hartford? The recreation department was strong. And so the Recreation Department they were there in Bellevue Square with tennis. At that time, Hartford's recreation department was strong all over the city, and the Blacks who were there who were in the leaders of the community, of course, they had to have the same thing that the South End had. You said the elites? What did you say? The Black intelligentsia. They wanted to have, you said, they wanted to have the same thing? They demanded. They demanded. And the powers that be provided the same, in the recreation department, facilities that they had down in the South End or the West End or wherever. But there was still that subtle but just as pernicious racism. You might say, why would you say that? Well, it was difficult to get a job in my generation. In fact, one fraternity Carpe Diem Fraternity, was established by educated Black folk to encourage Black males in Hartford to stay in school. When was Carpe Diem established? 1920. In the early 20s. There were a few. But see, what made what I think made a difference was the Great Migration that Isabel Wilkerson talks about, Seeking the Warmth of Other Suns. So there were smaller numbers. But when the Great Migration, of course, more people, more numbers and they weren't so isolated. There were some Black folks who lived on the South End. They had their own little quardrant down in the South End. Like Louis Peterson's family lived down in the South End, right? You've done your homework? Yeah. Gerrard Peterson. Gerrard Peterson today is up in Cape Cod, Martha's Vineyard, rather. He lives up there now. Okay. Those Negroes, I shouldn't say it that way. those Black folks who are early, they were small numbers, they were exposed to the same things that white immigrants were exposed to. What does it mean? It means that Gerrard Peterson became an Executive at Aetna Insurance Company. Which insurance company? Aetna. Aetna. It also meant that he became an Executive Director of the Hartford Civic Center. And it also meant that when he attended Bulkeley High School, it meant that he played golf at Bulkeley High School. At Bulkeley High School you were able to get an amateur license. At that time up at Keeney Park, the golf course, there was a group of Black folks, I can't remember the name, they'll come to me, but I don't play golf. The group of Black folks who formed their own golf in a municipal golf course that we all pay taxes to maintain. But that was not just indicative to Hartford. I found out. But here's my point, so Gerrard Peterson won on amateur golf because he knew how to play golf. They denied him. The city did. The city denied him. He was smart enough and he had the historical factors, to then city manager Eli Freeman knew his background, that he played golf and that he had been at Bulkeley High School. Very rare to see black folks out there. So what's the point? The point is that and it's siting in Hartford Magazine, not Hartford Magazine, what's the magazine that your's is in? The one about Bellevue Square? Connecticut Explored. Connecticut Explored did a story on it. On his exclusion? On how the whole thing went down. Oh, okay. How it went down. And I can remember because I was diagnosed with prostate cancer seven years ago, in the magazine it talked about that here in Hartford, Connecticut. The facts. But I remember. So did Eli Freeman intervene to overturn? Oh, yeah he overturned. He said "what are you?" But I think if anything, it's a human fear factor. I hate the terms like Uncle Toms and all that. It's a human fear factor. And so you made some changes. You made some positive upward mobility. But you hesitate to get into the fight with your fellow brothers and sisters. And I think that the Caribbean community struggles was that they were immigrants. They didn't experience Hartford as Black Americans did. They didn't experience Hartford. My father I told you was a laborer and he worked at a foundry in Rocky Hill. He would go to work at 3:00 in the morning. He would get stopped by cops. Finally my father said to them, "you've seen me before." He said, to the Black cops. "you know me. Mr. Bowden. You stopped me before you know who I am. Stop trying to impress these Irish cops." That was my father. Third grade education. No nonsense man who loved, who respected people. Did he work at the foundry until he died? Well, they eventually closed down the foundry because of the environmental problems. And then was he retired by then? Oh, no. What happened was he ended up getting a job. We were all grown kids then. He ended up getting a job in another foundry over on Homestead Avenue. Philbrick-Spencer. Got a job there. I know he also did some work for the guy who owned the place. He was a hard working man. My father worked six and a half days a week. I never saw, a hard working man, I never saw him put his hands on my mother. I saw my mother hit him. Did your mother work? My mother was busy having babies. My mother worked. Because there were ten of you, right? At that time, there were nine of us. Nine of you. My mother worked at the hospital in the kitchens. She also did housework. Her housework or you mean as a domestic? Domestic. We call we call them kitchen mechanics. But in West Hartford and in some places in Hartford, like on Scarborough Street. Oh, I'm proud of myself to remember a lot of stuff, but she's busy having kids. And then how long did you guys live in Bellevue Square? So you were born in Bellevue Square in '42. Lived in Bellevue Square into the 60s. Okay. When I graduated from Hartford Public High School. We can pause. Let me pause.

lines.line: # Introduction and Personal Background

> The speaker introduces himself as William M Brown, also known as Lew Brown, and shares his origins from Hartford. He mentions his middle name, Moses, and explains the evolution of his nickname from Luigi to Lew, reflecting on his identity and the influence of his birthplace on his life.

lines.line: # Parental Work, Family Structure, and Leaving Bellevue Square

> The speaker describes his father's work in foundries, his mother's role as a domestic worker and mother of many children, and the family's life in Bellevue Square. He notes the eventual closure of the foundry, his father's continued hard work, and the family's move out of Bellevue Square in the 1960s after he graduated from high school. The narrative provides insight into the family's work ethic, resilience, and transitions over time.

children.text: # Introduction and Name Origins

> The speaker introduces himself as William M. Brown, also known as Lew Brown, and explains the origins of his name and nickname. He discusses how his middle name, Moses, comes from family tradition, and how his nickname evolved from 'Luigi' to 'Lew' over the years, reflecting both street culture and ethnic associations. The conversation also touches on the significance of names within his family and community.
# Paternal Family History and Migration from South Carolina

> The speaker recounts his father's background, including his father's traumatic experience witnessing his own father's murder by white supremacists in Denmark, South Carolina. This event led to the family's migration north to Trenton, New Jersey, as a means of escaping racial violence and seeking safety. The narrative highlights the dangers faced by Black families in the South and the collective decision to leave for better opportunities.
# The Move to Trenton, New Jersey and Reflections on Trenton

> The discussion shifts to the family's move to Trenton, New Jersey, as part of the Great Migration. The speaker reflects on the reasons for choosing Trenton, the types of jobs available, and his own experiences living and working there. He describes the atmosphere of Trenton, the challenges he faced, and his eventual decision to leave, feeling a spiritual and personal disconnect with the city.
# Ancestry, Research, and Reflections on Hartford's Black History

> The speaker discusses the challenges of tracing his ancestry, particularly on his mother's side, and the importance of research in understanding family history. He reflects on the lack of historical documentation about Black Hartford, the significance of public housing developments like Bellevue Square, and the broader context of Black migration and settlement in the city. The conversation highlights the value of knowledge and the need for deeper historical inquiry.
# Family Reunion and Discovering Extended Family

> The speaker describes attending a family reunion in the Greater Trenton area, where he and his relatives from Hartford were warmly received by extended family from Delaware and Pennsylvania. The event revealed previously unknown connections and evoked strong emotions, as family members realized how far the Browns had migrated. The reunion underscored the importance of family ties and the sense of being part of a 'lost tribe' rediscovered.
# Maternal Family History and Georgia Roots

> Attention turns to the speaker's maternal lineage, rooted in Dawson, Georgia. He expresses regret over knowing little about this side of the family, noting the complexity of relationships and mixed heritage, including possible Native American ancestry. The speaker recalls visits to Georgia and the challenges of tracing family history due to fragmented records and blended family structures.
# Migration to Hartford and Early Family Life

> The narrative covers the family's move from Trenton to Hartford, Connecticut. The speaker explains that his mother moved first to stay with relatives, followed by his father who sought work in Hartford's foundries after struggling to find employment in Trenton. He describes his paternal grandmother as a strong, pipe-smoking woman who read the Bible daily and maintained a quiet but commanding presence in the family.
# Reflections on Black Political Power, Migration, and Community in Hartford

> The speaker and interviewer discuss the scarcity of research on Black Hartford, the intentional exclusion of Black history from mainstream narratives, and the evolution of political power among different immigrant groups in the city. The conversation touches on the roles of Black governors, ward politics, and the impact of migration from the South and the Caribbean. The speaker emphasizes the importance of understanding historical power dynamics and the interconnectedness of various communities in Hartford.
# Experiences as a Reporter and the 1968 Unrest in Hartford

> The speaker recounts his experiences as a reporter during the 1968 unrest following Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s assassination. He describes the heavy police presence, the physical boundaries set up to contain Black neighborhoods, and his realization that these boundaries mirrored a plantation system. The events served as a wake-up call about the realities of segregation and the limitations placed on Black communities in Hartford.
# Reflections on Cold Water Flats, Tenements, and Upward Mobility

> The speaker reflects on the transition from cold water flats and tenement buildings to Bellevue Square, noting that the move represented an upgrade for many families. He shares stories of other immigrant groups, such as Italians, who experienced similar hardships and aspirations for better living conditions. The narrative highlights the universal desire among parents to provide improved circumstances for their children and the empathy that can arise from shared struggles.
# Family Migration Patterns and the Carver Center in Trenton

> The conversation returns to the migration patterns of the speaker's family, including time spent in Trenton, New Jersey, and the significance of the Carver Center as a hub for the Black community. The speaker and interviewer discover a shared connection to Bellevue Avenue in Trenton, highlighting the small-world nature of their experiences. The discussion also touches on the importance of education and the challenges faced by Black families in different cities.
# Childhood in Bellevue Square and Community Life

> The speaker shares fond memories of growing up in Bellevue Square, a public housing development in Hartford. He recalls the strong sense of community, the support from neighbors like Mama Lou, and the positive environment despite the challenges of public housing. The narrative emphasizes the resilience and solidarity among residents, as well as the improvements Bellevue Square represented over previous living conditions.
# Health Challenges and Exposure to Broader Worlds

> The speaker recounts a significant period in his childhood when he was diagnosed with serious health issues, including heart, liver, and kidney trouble. He describes spending months in the hospital and a convalescent home, where he was exposed to people from different backgrounds and learned to judge others by their character. These experiences broadened his perspective and contributed to his appreciation for diversity and resilience.
# Return to School and Integration with Broader Hartford

> After recovering from illness, the speaker returns to school, attending special education classes at the James H. Naylor School, which served both Hartford and Wethersfield students. He describes being transported by limousine with other children with disabilities and the exposure to different communities and environments. The narrative also includes memories of summer camps and visits to estates, which further expanded his worldview.
# Daily Life, Play, and Recreation in Bellevue Square

> The speaker expresses his love for Hartford as a child, recalling the freedom to roam the city, the local stores where he bought candy, and the recreational facilities in Bellevue Square. He describes the playgrounds, splash pads, and the sense of safety and community that allowed children to play freely until the streetlights came on. These memories highlight the vibrancy and supportiveness of the neighborhood during his youth.
# Black Intelligentsia, Recreation, and Social Mobility

> The speaker discusses the role of the Black intelligentsia in advocating for equal recreational facilities and opportunities in Hartford. He describes the strong recreation department, the demands for parity with other neighborhoods, and the establishment of organizations to encourage Black youth to stay in school. The narrative underscores the importance of community leadership and the pursuit of social mobility through education and recreation.
# Black Professionals, Golf, and Barriers in Hartford

> The conversation turns to the experiences of Black professionals in Hartford, including exclusion from municipal golf courses and the efforts to challenge these barriers. The speaker recounts the story of Gerard Peterson, a Black executive who fought for access to the city's golf facilities, and the broader context of racial discrimination in professional and recreational spaces. The narrative highlights both the obstacles faced and the progress made by Black Hartford residents.
# Parental Work, Family Structure, and Leaving Bellevue Square

> The speaker describes his father's work in foundries, his mother's role as a domestic worker and mother of many children, and the family's life in Bellevue Square. He notes the eventual closure of the foundry, his father's continued hard work, and the family's move out of Bellevue Square in the 1960s after he graduated from high school. The narrative provides insight into the family's work ethic, resilience, and transitions over time.

story.description: In this interview, William M. Brown, also known as Lew Brown, shares personal stories and reflections on his experiences growing up as an African American in Hartford, Connecticut. Born in 1942, Brown discusses his family's roots, including his father's traumatic history in South Carolina and the family's migration north during the Great Migration. He talks about his upbringing in Bellevue Square, a public housing project in Hartford, highlighting the close-knit community, significant neighborhood figures, and the racial dynamics of the time. The narrative includes anecdotes of Brown's health struggles as a child, his educational journey, interactions with community leaders, and observations on race relations in Hartford. Brown reflects on the socio-political changes he's witnessed, mentioning his surprise at the lasting effects of gerrymandering and systemic racism. His experiences as a reporter and community member inform his perspective on racial identity, history, and hope for future generations.
